pageCaption.value: Billy Lee Lasley’s invention of a non-invasive method for determining the sex of birds — designed to help protect endangered species such as eagles — has slowly been adopted by researchers around the world. Perhaps more importantly, his work has proved to have other uses, including monitoring the reproductive health of women exposed to chemicals in the environment.

metadescription.value: Billy Lee Lasley’s invention of a non-invasive method for determining the sex of birds — designed to help protect endangered species such as eagles — has slowly been adopted by researchers around the world. Perhaps more importantly, his work has proved to have other uses, including monitoring the reproductive health of women exposed to chemicals in the environment.

pageTitle.value: Grégoire Courtine's Life in motion - Perpetual Planet - Video

pageCaption.value: Neuroscientist Grégoire Courtine's revolutionary "digital bridge" technology could change the lives of those with spinal cord injuries. Learn more on rolex.org.

pageCaption.value: Piyush Tewari is determined to save thousands of lives by ensuring that most accident victims in Delhi receive rapid medical care. His foundation, in partnership with hospitals and a medical science institute, has already provided training sessions in basic life support to over 2,000 police officers, as well as 500 ordinary citizens.

pageTitle.value: Andrew Bastawrous' Peek Vision - Perpetual Planet - Video

pageCaption.value: Andrew Bastawrous’ Peek Vision has screened 10 million people for sight issues across 12 countries since its founding. Watch the video on rolex.org.

pageCaption.value: For close to half a century, Luc Debecker, a Belgian-born industrial surveyor living in Switzerland and France, has been spending most of his weekends and holidays compiling a guide to the world’s oldest "art gallery" — the caves where Stone Age peoples living in Europe 40,000 years ago painted the animals of their time with astonishing realism and skill.

pageTitle.value: Protect a giant fish for the Amazon - Video

pageCaption.value: The largest scaled freshwater fish in the world – the giant arapaima – is bound for extinction. But in a close partnership with local associations and fishing leaders, fisheries ecologist João Campos-Silva has a plan to save not only the arapaima but with it, the livelihoods, food supply and culture of the indigenous communities who depend on the Amazon’s rivers for survival.
